text: The surgeon begins a robotic right indirect inguinal hernia repair by making a vertical oblique incision lateral to the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS). The incision is guided by an island of fat, serving as a landmark. The surgeon aims for approximately four centimeters of overlap lateral to the internal ring, navigating between the retroactive space and the space of Bogros, while preserving preperitoneal fat near the peritoneum.

The surgeon uses the left hand for effective retraction, creating a cotton candy-like appearance of the tissue to aid in dissection and maintain hemostasis. The focus is on achieving a precise dissection near the peritoneum, with careful manipulation to enter the correct anatomical plane.

The surgeon uses cautery to enter the correct plane posteriorly, cutting the transverse salus sling around the vas deferens, which is not currently visible. The patient's tissues appear thin with minimal fat, requiring precise dissection. The surgeon separates the testicular vessels from the hernia sac using robotic precision, ensuring a bloodless field and clear tissue planes. The dissection continues around the hernia sac to remove fatty tissue and small vessels.

The surgeon uses cautery to carefully dissect small vessels from the hernia sac, revealing the tip of the sac. The testicular vessels are meticulously divided from the sac, allowing it to be gradually extracted from the internal ring. The surgeon continues to work broadly, dividing non-white attachments to facilitate the sac's removal.

The surgeon uses good retraction with the left hand to manage aerial tissue, ensuring no bleeding occurs during the procedure. The mesh is positioned to cross the midline, extending two centimeters deep into the pelvis. The surgeon carefully preserves the vessel named Nolan, staying behind it when possible. The pubis is visible as the surgeon crosses the midline. The external iliac artery is identified, and the surgeon parietalizes the cord by peeling the peritoneum off the testicular vessels and vas deferens to ensure proper mesh placement.

The surgeon uses robotic precision to dissect medial to the external iliac vein, identifying an aberrant vessel connecting with the obturator vessels. A fascial plane is visible over the iliac artery, iliac vein, and lateral structures including the genital branch, genitofemoral nerve, and femoral branch, which helps prevent direct mesh contact and potential irritation or erosion.

The surgeon replaces the scissors with a needle driver and adjusts the pre-placed mesh and sutures in the abdomen. The mesh is positioned past the midline, ensuring it remains open and properly placed. The surgeon notes the pocket is small and uses a grasper and needle driver for blunt dissection to create more space. The mesh is tucked into the retropubic space, with efforts to align the vertical blue line over the vas deferens. The surgeon identifies and carefully dissects peritoneum that was tucked behind the mesh to prevent it from sneaking behind.

The surgeon performs additional dissection medially using two graspers and a needle driver to ensure the mesh is properly tucked in. The mesh is positioned with coverage over the myopectineal orifice, and a 3-0 vicryl stitch is placed to Cooper's ligament to prevent mesh displacement as the bladder rises. Another stitch is placed superior laterally to prevent counterclockwise rotation of the mesh and to stop the peritoneum from sneaking behind the mesh posterior laterally.

The surgeon prepares to close the peritoneal flap using a 3-0 V-lock suture, starting laterally. The needle is kept in the right hand to maintain efficiency during the closure. The surgeon carefully aligns the edges of the lower flap to avoid excessive tension, ensuring the peritoneal flap is not too taut.

The surgeon uses a zero-degree scope to view the surgical field, opting for its efficiency and clarity over angled scopes. A small hole in the hernia sac is closed using a figure-of-eight stitch with a 3-0 vicryl suture.
The surgeon inspects the lower edge of the mesh to ensure there is no peritoneum sneaking behind it, preventing any potential posterior lateral recurrence.
